sentences.text: [BEEPING]
My name is Adam Goszer, G-O-S-Z-E-R.
My birth name is G-O-S-Z-E-R, Goszer.
And first name is Abraham.
August the 15th, 1921.
Korzec, K-O-R-Z-E-C.
Korzec was a small town.
We were about approximately 7,000 Jews in the city.
It was a Ukrainian territory, actually.
But we used to do business with the Ukrainian.
The Ukrainian people used to do business with us, like, with my father.
I was too young at the time.
And we got along very good.
Jewish life.
Jewish life, we had many synagogues in our city, or town.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] many Jewish organizations.
It was actually a Zionist town.
Myself, I used to belong to organization called Betar, all my life.
But there were others.
There were [HEBREW], Halutzim.
And we had sports clubs.
Like myself, I used to belong to a sports club.
I used to play soccer.
And I was an amateur boxer.
My family.
My father died in 1932.
And my mother was left with three children.
Like, myself, my brother, and my sister, the immediate family.
And it was hard for a widow to raise children, but she somehow managed.
Myself, I went to a Hebrew school called Bet Sefer Tarbut.
My brother went to the same school, Bet Sefer Tarbut, but then he went to yeshiva, because-- because we had a famous yeshiva in our town.
My sister was the youngest one.
She went to same school, to Bet Sefer Tarbut.
It was a private school.
No, I was the middle one.
My brother was the oldest, then me, then my sister.
Well, it was difficult for a widow to raise children.
But she managed, like I said.
And we had everything, whatever we needed to immediate life.
We had our own house.
And my father actually left some money before he passed away, and she managed to raise us.
And there we are.
My father was a grain buyer.
My father had a large family on his side, a number of brothers, and they used to buy grain.
And after that, they used to ship it to Poland, to central Poland.
Well, like I said before, we were with Ukrainian people.
So I had a lot of friends from school.
But at the same time, I had a lot friends, Ukrainian friends, because we used to belong to the same clubs.
Yes, the majority were Jewish friends.
But, again, I used to go out with a lot of Ukrainian friends.
At home, Yiddish.
And second was Ukrainian.
I, as a youngster, did not.
But there was anti-Semitism for sure.
But I didn't feel it.
Yes, my father used to go every Saturday, every holiday.
As a matter of fact, he used to take me with him all the time, and we used to go to synagogue.
And everybody had their own synagogues, like, class of people.
We went to a synagogue was called The Rabbi's Synagogue.
Yeah.
The most was in Hebrew.
And-- but we used to take a few classes in Polish, too, because it was compulsory in-- to live in Poland, it was compulsory for the schools.
But we used to speak in school all the time Hebrew.
Yes, I had a good life.
We had everything in the house.
I used to have my own bicycle.
My brother had a motorcycle.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a lot of friends.
And we used to play soccer in summer.
We used to play soccer, like, against other schools, because it was not professional.
It was amateur.
The war started actually September 1, 1939, when the Germans attacked Poland.
And we heard a lot of rumors that the Germans are doing terrible things to the Jews.
So we were afraid, but we didn't have any alternative to run anywhere, because we used to live right on the border, Poland-Russia.
Like, from my home to the border was maybe 500 meters.
And then the Germans attacked Poland.
And they took Poland almost in two weeks.
The 17th of September, the Russians crossed the border and occupied our city, Korzec, And they went farther south.
But living under the Russian laws, it wasn't the same as for Poland.
But again, we had everything.
Nobody suffered.
My brother was a professional photographer before the war.
So when the Soviet Union came, they made collective photographers.
Like, they took one studio, and they employed all the photographers.
And they used to pay them whatever the wage was at that time.
I did not have a profession, so my brother took me in to learn photography.
And I worked there till the Germans attacked Russia.
And they attacked Russia the 22nd of June, 1941.
No, because I went to Hebrew school, and that was only seven grades.
And when the Russians came in, we had to go to work.
And I had to learn a profession at that time, or a trade.
But life was going on steadily.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not as we used to have everything for Poland, but, again, there was nothing to complain.
Until the Germans attacked the Soviet Union.
And that was 1941, the 22nd of June.
And in no time, they came to our city, Korzec.
When they came, everybody was afraid and everybody was sitting in their homes.
But then the Ukraines, right away, they gained power, the laws.
And the more educated Ukrainians, one of them, for an example, became mayor of the city.
And he was a very, very bad man.
And then a young fellow, Ukrainian fellow, used to work for a Jewish bakery.
So he knew a little bit Jewish.
So he told that he knows German, so he became the chief of the police.
And they used to go around the Jewish houses, streets, catch young people, older people, and put them to work.
Like, when the army-- the German army moved farther north, but they used to stop at night in the city, in the parks, and so on.
So they used to run wild, just like murders.
Catch anybody on the street, or break the doors, come in and pull you out, and you didn't know where they taking you.
And that was going on--
The Ukrainian police.
Because most Germans didn't know if you're Jewish or you're Pole or you're Ukrainian.
But the-- the local police knew who is Jewish, because they recognized Jewish people.
And they put them up to work on the streets to clean the streets, to cut wood.
They took, for an example-- the Ukrainians-- they took Jewish girls for maids to work for them to wash floors.
Elderly people, when they couldn't do this kind of a work, they used to put them up on the street to sweep the streets, to clean the streets.
And you had to actually have somewhere a job at a German place, that he gave you a bescheinigung, a certificate, that you're employed at a German.
Then the police would not catch you or take you.
And that was going on the first couple weeks.
Then they put up a law strictly for Jews.
The Jews cannot walk on the street after, let's say, after 8 o'clock in the evening in summer.
Jews have to wear yellow patches on the front and in the back.
And you have to put up a star-- a Star of David paint on your house that they will recognize which one is Jewish.
To go in the market to buy something, they gave you just certain hours-- from 8:00 in the morning till 10:00.
And if you didn't follow their rules and regulation-- and their laws, the death penalty-- you'd get the death penalty.
Yes, we still lived in our homes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was in 1941.
In 1941, when winter came, it was a very, very cold winter.
So the Germans came to the Judenrat, and they asked that we should give away all our winter clothes, like our furs and gloves and wood, because they have to send it to the front for the Germans.
And so we were left without warm clothes.
And there was no wood to heat the houses.
So we used to take demolished old houses and get the wood and burn in-- in the stoves to make sure that we get a little bit of heat.
Then they used to give us 200, 250 grams of bread.
So you've-- only a working person.
So let's say if I was working, I got 250 grams.
But we were three people, because my older brother was in the Russian army.
So it wasn't enough.
Labor.
Strictly labor, At the beginning.
Then, in 1942, everybody in the city said, if you work in the sugar factory-- we had a big sugar factory-- that the [INAUDIBLE] had a concession of all the sugar factories.
So I went to work for the sugar factory.
And again, labor.
But at least you had a certificate that you're working in the sugar factory for the Germans.
And we used to receive 250 grams bread.
But we used to get a little bit of molasses.
And the molasses, we used to trade with the farmers, because they used to make homebrew from it.
So it helped us a little bit.
About that time, I was about 18, 19 years old.
Well, what we used to do-- first of all, we used to go to work.
When we came from work, there was nothing actually to do, because we couldn't go on the street.
So we used to get together, a few fellows, in a basement or in a-- a house, and talk politics.
And the politics was, will we survive?
Would the Germans survive?
Would they take Russia?
My personal opinion at that time that nobody in history ever won a war from the Soviet Union.
And I believed in it.
I remember once I was in a village, and an old Ukrainian fellow-- he was at that time maybe 70, 75-- and the army was moving, day and night.
And he told me, he says, see these soldiers moving, so many of them?
They won't-- 10% won't return from them.
They'll die in Russia.
They'll get killed.
And I believed in it, so we lived only with hope something is going to change.
We didn't know anything, because all the radios were confiscated by the Germans and the Ukrainian police.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only thing we knew, that the army's moving-- the German propaganda-- that they're destroying Russia in another two weeks, another two weeks.
Particularly as they went deeper.
It was worse for them.
Yeah.
My mother was a housewife.
Whatever she was able to-- to-- to cook, to bake, to make something.
My sister was still young.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So whatever we used to bring home, my mother used to put together, make dinner or whatever.
There wasn't enough, but we survived.
Then came 1942, spring.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And each time the Gestapo used to come to the city, there was all the time rumors that they're-- they're going to kill Jews.
They're going to kill Jews.
In the-- actual, they used to start early in the morning.
So when we knew a day before that they're going to kill, some people escaped to the forest, some to the farmers.
Some had hidings at home.
But eventually, the Ukrainian police did find out that Jews disappear, so nothing happened.
So it was quiet for a day till all the Jews returned back to the city.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
In 1942, in May, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Erev Shavuot, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we got rumors that Ukrainian police from different towns and cities came to the city.
That was on a Wednesday.
I remember the day.
So some of Jews right away got scared, and they run to the field or the forest.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then the day of Shavuot, they surrounded the whole city.
And they went from house to house-- because every house had a Star of David.
They knew which one-- and they pulled out young and old and children, women.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they took them away to the Ukrainian police, because there was a big market, and they put them up all on that market.
And they had to go into the police and register themself-- their name, age, address.
And whatever they have on them, the police took it away.
And they put up in groups of 60 people.
And they took them away to a forest.
There was a village called Kozak, K-O-Z-A-K.
And there were pits dug, two big ones.
And as they took them there in groups of 60s to the pits, there were a few Ukrainian police.
And there were a few Gestapo ones.
And that day, Shavuot, they killed-- shot in cold blood-- 2,200 people. 1,600 women and children and 600 men.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, before they came to the pit, about 500 meters, they stopped.
They undressed them.
They took all the clothes.
And the people went naked to the pit.
And when they came there, they shot them.
And they were killing up to 4 o'clock in the afternoon.
If they brought you from the city after 4:00, you survived.
They wouldn't kill you anymore.
So they killed at that time 2,200 people.
Women and children were 16-- [VOICE BREAKS] [SOBBING] [SNIFFLING] [CLEARS THROAT] And then-- and then the rest of these survived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They came back to the city, and the Gestapo and the police said, from now on, you can move over on one street.
And you can start going back to work.
And there would be no more killings.
I was at home with my family-- my mother, my sister, and my brother.
We had in our home-- we had a basement.
And we made a other basement under that basement that in case anything, we run in, and we hide there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's where we-- the day of Shavuot-- we were hiding.
OK.
But after 4 o'clock, they had cars with loud speakers.
Any Jew is hidden, any Jew is awake, you can come back to the city-- we won't kill you anymore-- and start your normal life again.
And that's what it was.
We came back, got out from the hidings.
People what they were in forest, they came back.
And the Germans said at that time, and the Ukrainian police, now, why don't you move over on one street so you'll be close all together?
Because there was no family that was not affected by the killing-- a mother, a brother, a sister, a father.
So we moved over on one street.
OK.
Let's stop here.
Mr.
Goszer, you can drink.
